heb.paragraph: The wordplay between pistia (faith/faithfulness) and apistia (unfaith/unfaithfulness) in vv. 3–4 is central. Paul asks: if some Jews were unfaithful (ēpistēsan), will their unfaithfulness (apistia) nullify God’s faithfulness (pistin tou theou)? The answer is emphatic: mē genoito (‘by no means!’). God’s covenant fidelity does not depend on human performance.

panels.ctx: Romans 3:1–8 addresses the logical objection arising from chapter 2: if external circumcision and law-possession confer no advantage, what is the value of being Jewish at all? Paul affirms that there is ‘much in every way’ (v. 2) — primarily that the Jews were entrusted with the ‘very words of God’ (ta logia tou theou). The passage is the first instance of the mē genoito (‘by no means!’) formula that punctuates Romans at key turning points (3:4, 6, 31; 6:2, 15; 7:7, 13; 9:14; 11:1, 11).

cross.note: Paul quotes David’s confession that God is ‘justified when he speaks and prevails when he judges’ — human sin cannot impugn God’s righteousness.
If we are faithless, God remains faithful, for he cannot deny himself — the same principle Paul articulates.

notes.note: The advantage of the Jew is real and significant: they were entrusted with God’s oracles (ta logia). This includes the entire Old Testament revelation, the covenants, and the messianic promises.
Paul’s emphatic mē genoito (‘may it never be!’) is the strongest form of denial in Koine Greek. God’s faithfulness is the immovable foundation on which salvation rests.

The objection — ‘if our unrighteousness highlights God’s righteousness, is God unjust to punish us?’ — is a reductio ad absurdum. Paul refuses to engage it except to denounce its logic as worthy of condemnation.
Calvin sees the oracles of God as the supreme advantage of Israel. All other privileges flow from this one: the law, the prophets, and the promises are all contained in the revealed Word.
God must be found true even if it means every human is a liar. Calvin insists that divine veracity is the non-negotiable axiom of all theology.
The word logia (‘oracles’) refers broadly to God’s revealed words, encompassing law, prophecy, and covenant promises. The term carries connotations of divine authority and trustworthiness.
Paul’s dismissal of the ‘let us do evil that good may come’ argument anticipates the fuller treatment in 6:1–2, where he addresses the same antinomian objection.

Moo notes that this passage inaugurates a pattern of objection-and-response that structures much of Romans. Paul anticipates the questions his argument will provoke and addresses them prophylactically. The key theological affirmation is that God’s faithfulness to Israel is unconditional and irrevocable.

Schreiner argues that ‘the oracles of God’ include the covenant promises to Abraham, making this verse a bridge to the argument of chapter 4. Israel’s unfaithfulness cannot undo what God promised to Abraham.

heb.paragraph: The phrase erga nomou (works of the law) in v. 20 is one of the most debated expressions in Pauline scholarship. The traditional reading takes it as any attempt to earn righteousness by law-keeping. The New Perspective (Dunn, Wright) limits it to Jewish identity markers (circumcision, food laws, Sabbath) that function as ethnic boundary markers. Most scholars now recognise both dimensions: the phrase includes but is not limited to identity markers.

panels.ctx: Romans 3:9–20 is Paul’s devastating summation of human sinfulness. The catena of Old Testament quotations (vv. 10–18, drawn from Psalms 14, 53, 5, 140, 10, Isaiah 59, and Psalm 36) is the longest such chain in the Pauline corpus. The quotations are carefully arranged: they move from the general indictment (‘none righteous,’ v. 10) to sins of speech (vv. 13–14) to sins of violence (vv. 15–17) to the root cause (‘no fear of God,’ v. 18). The conclusion in v. 20 is the hinge of the entire argument: ‘by the works of the law no one will be justified in his sight.’

cross.note: The primary source for the ‘none righteous’ catena — the psalmist’s universal indictment of human corruption.
Paul makes the same declaration in Galatians: no one is justified by works of the law but through faith in Jesus Christ.
Paul’s language in v. 20 echoes the psalmist’s plea: ‘no one living is righteous before you.’

notes.note: Paul’s verdict: Jews and Gentiles are both ‘under sin.’ The preposition hypo (under) suggests dominion — sin reigns as a power over all humanity.
The catena of OT quotations is a courtroom exhibit. Paul summons Scripture itself as the prosecuting witness, ensuring that the indictment cannot be dismissed as his personal opinion.
The whole world ‘held accountable to God’ — hypodikos, a legal term meaning ‘subject to the court’s verdict.’ Every mouth is stopped; every defence is silenced.
The law’s purpose is diagnostic, not curative. Through the law comes ‘consciousness of sin’ (epignōsis hamartias), not deliverance from it.
Calvin insists that ‘none righteous’ admits of no exception. The statement is universal and applies to every human being apart from the imputed righteousness of Christ.
The law cannot justify because it was never designed to justify. Its function is to expose sin, not to remedy it. Calvin calls this the ‘pedagogical’ use of the law.
The verb proechometha is debated: ‘are we [Jews] any better off?’ or ‘do we have any advantage?’ Paul’s answer (‘not at all’) qualifies the ‘much in every way’ of 3:2.
The phrase ‘by observing the law’ translates ex ergōn nomou. The preposition ex indicates source or basis: no one is declared righteous on the basis of law performance.

Moo reads this section as the conclusion of the argument begun in 1:18. The OT catena functions as a scriptural verdict confirming Paul’s theological argument: all humanity, Jew and Gentile alike, stands condemned before God’s bar.

Moo takes ‘works of the law’ broadly as any human attempt to achieve right standing with God through law-keeping, not narrowly as Jewish identity markers. The phrase’s scope is universal, not ethnically limited.

Schreiner sees vv. 19–20 as the climax of 1:18–3:20 — the entire section demonstrates that humanity is universally sinful and that the law, far from providing a remedy, only deepens the awareness of guilt. The stage is now set for the ‘but now’ of 3:21.

heb.paragraph: Hilastērion in v. 25 is one of the most contested words in Romans. In the LXX it translates the Hebrew kapporet, the ‘mercy seat’ — the gold lid on the Ark of the Covenant where the Day of Atonement blood was sprinkled (Lev 16:14–15). If Paul intends this allusion, Christ is the new mercy seat — the place where God’s wrath meets God’s mercy. Alternatively, hilastērion may mean ‘propitiation’ (appeasing wrath) or ‘expiation’ (removing guilt). The debate is significant but not exclusive: Christ’s death both satisfies divine justice and cleanses human sin.

The verb dikaióō is a forensic term from the courtroom: to pronounce a favourable verdict, to declare righteous. Paul uses it in v. 24 with the qualifying adverb dōrean (‘freely,’ ‘as a gift’), emphasising that justification is not earned but received. The passive voice (dikaiomenoi, ‘being justified’) indicates that God is the agent; humans are the recipients.

panels.ctx: Romans 3:21–31 is the theological centre of the epistle and one of the most important passages in the New Testament. The phrase ‘but now’ (nyni de) in v. 21 marks the great turning point from condemnation to justification, from the revelation of wrath (1:18) to the revelation of righteousness. Luther called these verses the ‘chief part of this epistle and of the whole Bible.’ The dense theological vocabulary — righteousness, justification, redemption, propitiation, faith, grace, law — is packed into eleven verses that effectively summarise the entire gospel.

cross.note: The Day of Atonement ritual — the priest sprinkles blood on the mercy seat (hilastērion). Paul’s use of this term casts Christ’s death as the ultimate Day of Atonement.
Salvation by grace through faith, not by works — the most compact parallel to Paul’s declaration of justification in Romans 3.
Paul’s parallel declaration that justification comes through faith in Christ, not by works of the law.

notes.note: The pivotal ‘but now’ (nyni de) introduces the solution to the problem of 1:18–3:20. After establishing universal condemnation, Paul reveals universal provision.
All have sinned (hēmarton, aorist) and fall short (hysterountai, present) of God’s glory. The combination of tenses suggests a past definitive act with ongoing consequences.
Three words define justification: dōrean (‘freely’), chariti (‘by his grace’), and apolytrōseōs (‘through redemption’). The gift is free, the motive is grace, and the means is redemption in Christ.
God presented Christ as a hilastērion — a sacrifice of atonement. This was public, deliberate, and efficacious: God himself provided the sacrifice that satisfies his own justice.
The conclusion: a person is justified by faith ‘apart from the works of the law.’ This verse is the Reformation’s magna carta.

Calvin insists that ‘freely’ (dōrean) excludes all human merit. Justification is entirely gratuitous — it originates in grace, is accomplished in Christ, and is received by faith. No human work contributes anything to it.
Calvin reads hilastērion as ‘propitiation’ — God’s wrath against sin is real and must be satisfied. Christ’s death is the satisfaction of divine justice, not merely an example of sacrificial love.

The term hilastērion may be translated ‘mercy seat’ (following the LXX usage) or ‘propitiation’ (following the broader semantic range). The NET Bible notes that the Day of Atonement imagery is likely primary, with Christ as the place where God’s wrath is satisfied and sin is removed.
The word ‘apart from’ (chōris) is a strong exclusion. Justification and works of the law occupy separate spheres in Paul’s soteriology.

Moo identifies this as the theological epicentre of the letter. The ‘righteousness of God’ revealed here is both God’s saving activity and the status he bestows on believers. The ‘apart from the law’ clause does not mean ‘contrary to the law’ but ‘by a different route than law-keeping.’ The law and prophets themselves ‘testify’ to this righteousness (v. 21).

Moo favours ‘propitiation’ for hilastērion, arguing that the context of God’s wrath (1:18; 3:5) demands a term that addresses the divine anger, not merely human sin. Christ’s death satisfies God’s justice while demonstrating his mercy.

Schreiner argues that the ‘but now’ of v. 21 is both logical and temporal: it marks a new era in salvation history. The righteousness of God is now revealed through the cross in a way it was not previously available. The participial clause ‘being justified freely’ (dikaioumenoi dōrean) defines justification as a gift, not an achievement.
Schreiner reads ‘the law of faith’ (nomos pisteōs) not as a principle but as the Mosaic law rightly understood — the law itself, when properly read, points to faith as the means of righteousness.

rows.label: vv.1–8
rows.text: Objection: What Advantage Has the Jew?

rows.label: vv.21–26
rows.text: The Centre: Justified Freely Through Redemption

rows.label: vv.27–31
rows.text: Conclusion: Faith, Not Works; One God for All

lit.note: Romans 3 pivots from universal condemnation to universal provision. The ‘but now’ of v. 21 is the great hinge of the entire letter, introducing the solution (justification by grace through faith) to the problem established in 1:18–3:20.

themes.note: Faith peaks at 10: this is the chapter where justification by faith is definitively stated. Justice at 9 reflects the forensic categories of justification. Mercy at 8 captures the gracious character of the ‘free gift.’

hebtext.note: The wordplay between pistia (faith/faithfulness) and apistia (unfaith/unfaithfulness) in vv. 3–4 is central. Paul asks: if some Jews were unfaithful (ēpistēsan), will their unfaithfulness (apistia) nullify God’s faithfulness (pistin tou theou)? The answer is emphatic: mē genoito (‘by no means!’). God’s covenant fidelity does not depend on human performance.

The phrase erga nomou (works of the law) in v. 20 is one of the most debated expressions in Pauline scholarship. The traditional reading takes it as any attempt to earn righteousness by law-keeping. The New Perspective (Dunn, Wright) limits it to Jewish identity markers (circumcision, food laws, Sabbath) that function as ethnic boundary markers. Most scholars now recognise both dimensions: the phrase includes but is not limited to identity markers.

Hilastērion in v. 25 is one of the most contested words in Romans. In the LXX it translates the Hebrew kapporet, the ‘mercy seat’ — the gold lid on the Ark of the Covenant where the Day of Atonement blood was sprinkled (Lev 16:14–15). If Paul intends this allusion, Christ is the new mercy seat — the place where God’s wrath meets God’s mercy. Alternatively, hilastērion may mean ‘propitiation’ (appeasing wrath) or ‘expiation’ (removing guilt). The debate is significant but not exclusive: Christ’s death both satisfies divine justice and cleanses human sin.

The verb dikaióō is a forensic term from the courtroom: to pronounce a favourable verdict, to declare righteous. Paul uses it in v. 24 with the qualifying adverb dōrean (‘freely,’ ‘as a gift’), emphasising that justification is not earned but received. The passive voice (dikaiomenoi, ‘being justified’) indicates that God is the agent; humans are the recipients.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: The statement is universal and applies to every human being apart from the imputed righteousness of Christ…

ppl.name: Isaiah
ppl.text: 10–18, drawn from Psalms 14, 53, 5, 140, 10, Isaiah 59, and Psalm 36) is the longest such chain in the Pauline corpus…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Righteousness Through Faith

translations.text: What shall we conclude then? Do we have any advantage? Not at all! For we have already made the charge that Jews and Gentiles alike are all under the power of sin.
What then? Are we Jews any better off? No, not at all. For we have already charged that all, both Jews and Greeks, are under sin,
Someone might argue, “If my falsehood enhances God’s truthfulness and so increases his glory, why am I still condemned as a sinner?”
But if through my lie God’s truth abounds to his glory, why am I still being condemned as a sinner?

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "The advantage of the Jew is real and significant: they were entrusted with God’s oracles (ta logia). This includes the entire Old Testament revelation, the covenants, and the messianic promises."

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin sees the oracles of God as the supreme advantage of Israel. All other privileges flow from this one: the law, the prophets, and the promises are all contained in the revealed Word."

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The word logia (‘oracles’) refers broadly to God’s revealed words, encompassing law, prophecy, and covenant promises. The term carries connotations of divine authority and trustworthiness."

debate: The word logia (‘oracles’) refers broadly to God’s revealed words, encompassing law, prophecy, and covenant promises. The term carries connotations of…
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Righteousness Through Faith within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: Paul quotes David’s confession that God is ‘justified when he speaks and prevails when he judges’ — human sin cannot impugn God’s righteousness.
If we are faithless, God remains faithful, for he cannot deny himself — the same principle Paul articulates.
The primary source for the ‘none righteous’ catena — the psalmist’s universal indictment of human corruption.
Paul makes the same declaration in Galatians: no one is justified by works of the law but through faith in Jesus Christ.
Paul’s language in v. 20 echoes the psalmist’s plea: ‘no one living is righteous before you.’

discourse.thesis: All have sinned and fall short of God's glory, but are justified freely by grace through faith in Christ.

nodes.text: What advantage has the Jew? Much in every way — they were entrusted with the oracles of God. Does their unfaithfulness nullify God's faithfulness? By no means!

children.text: Let God be true though every man be false — citing Psalm 51:4 to affirm God's righteousness in judgment.

nodes.text: Anticipated objection: if our unrighteousness highlights God's righteousness, is God unjust to inflict wrath? By no means! — that would make God unfit to judge the world.
Are we Jews any better off? Not at all — all, Jews and Greeks, are under sin. Scripture proves it: 'None is righteous, no not one.'

children.text: Paul chains together OT texts (Ps 14, 5, 140, 10; Prov 1; Isa 59) as a catena of universal guilt — throat, tongue, lips, mouth, feet, eyes, all corrupted.

nodes.text: The law speaks to those under the law so that every mouth is stopped and the whole world held accountable to God. No one is justified by works of the law — the law reveals sin.

THE TURNING POINT: God's righteousness has been revealed apart from law — righteousness through faith in Jesus Christ for all who believe. All have sinned; all are justified freely by grace through redemption in Christ Jesus.

children.text: God put Christ forward as a propitiation (ἱλαστήριον) to demonstrate his righteousness — passing over former sins and proving that he is both just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus.

nodes.text: Where is boasting? Excluded — by the 'law of faith.' A person is justified by faith apart from works of the law. God is God of Gentiles too. Do we nullify the law? By no means — we uphold it.

discourse.note: Romans 3:21 marks the pivotal νυνὶ δέ ('but now') — the transition from diagnosis (1:18–3:20) to remedy (3:21–4:25). The ἱλαστήριον language evokes the Day of Atonement mercy seat.
